summary:
Gobekli Tepe in southeastern Anatolia is one of the most important archaeological discoveries of the 20th century. Phase B, dating to approximately 8000–7000 BCE, followed the earlier and grander Phase A, and is characterized by smaller, rectangular enclosures that replaced the large circular structures of the earlier phase. The T-shaped limestone pillars that define the site continued to be carved with relief images of animals, abstract symbols, and anthropomorphic forms, though the later enclosures appear less monumentally constructed than the earlier ones.

The significance of Gobekli Tepe lies in its demonstration that complex ritual architecture and organized communal labor were present among pre-agricultural or early agricultural communities, predating cities by thousands of years. The deliberate backfilling of the Phase A enclosures — which took considerable effort — and the construction of Phase B facilities suggests an ongoing sacred use of the site over multiple generations. Gobekli Tepe has prompted substantial revision of the relationship between ritual complexity and the development of settled agricultural life.
